turns.content: I'm trying to build up my vinyl collection with some rare dancehall pressings but I don't even know where to START. like where do people find this stuff in 2025??

Great quest to be on! Rare dancehall vinyl can be tricky to track down because a lot of it was pressed in small runs in Jamaica and never widely distributed. Here are the main channels:

**Online:**
- **Discogs** is your best friend -- the marketplace has sellers worldwide with dancehall 7" singles, riddim compilations, and rare LPs. You can set up wantlists and get notified when specific pressings show up.
- **eBay** still turns up gems, especially from sellers clearing out old collections. Search for specific riddim names or producer names rather than just "dancehall vinyl."
- **Bandcamp** -- some reissue labels press limited dancehall runs there.

**In-person in Toronto:**
- **Kops Records** (Bloor West) has a decent reggae/dancehall section.
- **Sonic Boom** (Spadina) -- hit or miss but they get interesting stuff in.
- **Rotate This** (Queen West) -- smaller but curated, sometimes has Caribbean music.

What era and style are you looking for specifically? That'll help narrow things down.

I'm looking for late 80s to mid 90s digital dancehall stuff!! like the Sleng Teng riddim era, Bobby Digital productions, Steely & Clevie. those producers were making MAGIC with basic equipment still. the sound was so raw and the 45s from that era are just chef's kiss!!

Oh you have TASTE. That golden era of digital dancehall is some of the most influential music ever made. The Sleng Teng riddim alone changed everything -- Casio MT-40 into a mixing board and it rewired the whole genre.

For that specific era, here's what I'd recommend:

- **Search Discogs by riddim name** -- "Sleng Teng," "Stalag," "Real Rock" (the digital versions), "Punaany," "Bam Bam." The riddim-based search will turn up dozens of different voicings on each one.
- **Bobby Digital productions** -- search for "Bobby Digital" or "Robert Dixon" (his real name) on Discogs. His Digital-B label pressings are collectible but still findable.
- **Steely & Clevie** -- look for their Steely & Clevie label imprint. Their stuff turns up more often than you'd think because they were prolific.
- **Look for the original Jamaican label pressings** -- Jammys, Penthouse, Digital-B, Techniques, Star Trail. These are worth more than later reissues and they sound better.

Price-wise, common voicings on popular riddims go for $5-15. Rare tunes or DJ specials can be $30-100+.

the JAMMYS label!! yes!! King Jammys is the godfather of digital dancehall. I want some original Jammys pressings BAD. like Under Mi Sleng Teng by Wayne Smith on original press?? that would be a GRAIL piece for real!!

An original 1985 Jammys pressing of "Under Mi Sleng Teng" is definitely a grail piece -- that record literally changed music history. A clean copy can go for $50-150 depending on condition, but they do come up on Discogs a few times a year.

Tips for spotting legit original Jammys pressings:

- **Check the label design** -- original Jammys 7"s have a distinctive label style. The early-to-mid 80s ones usually have a simple text layout with "King Jammys" or "Jammys Records" and the Jamaica address.
- **Matrix/run-out numbers** -- the etchings in the dead wax near the label. Original Jamaican pressings often have hand-etched matrix numbers. Compare them to verified copies on Discogs.
- **Vinyl weight and quality** -- Jamaican pressings from that era tend to be lighter weight than US or UK pressings. That's normal, not a defect.
- **Condition grading** -- with Caribbean vinyl, anything VG+ or above is a win. The climate in Jamaica is hard on vinyl, so pristine copies are rare.

Don't sleep on the B-sides either -- Jammys 45s often have a version/dub on the flip that's just as good as the vocal side.

the dub versions!! YES!! sometimes the B-side dub is even BETTER than the A-side. like the stripped back instrumental lets you hear what the producer was really doing with the riddim. that's where the genius is!!

100%. The dub/version side is where you hear the producer's fingerprints most clearly. All the echo throws, the filter sweeps, the way they strip instruments in and out -- that's pure production artistry. Guys like King Tubby, Scientist, and then Jammys carrying that tradition into the digital era.

If you love the dub sides, also look for:
- **"Dub plates"** -- one-off acetates that were cut for specific sound systems. These are extremely rare and expensive ($200+) but they do surface occasionally. They're the original exclusive remix.
- **Greensleeves Records** dub compilations from the late 80s/90s -- they licensed a lot of Jamaican productions for UK release and the pressings are usually better quality vinyl.
- **VP Records** reissues -- they've been reissuing a lot of classic riddim compilations. Not original pressings, but affordable ways to get the music on wax.

Building a dancehall vinyl collection is a marathon, not a sprint. Set up those Discogs wantlists, check in weekly, and be patient. The gems turn up when you least expect them.

ok I just made a Discogs wantlist with like 30 tunes on it lol. my wallet is NOT ready for this hobby but you know what, these records are part of HISTORY still. some of these riddims shaped everything I do as a producer. it's like owning a piece of the blueprint!!

That's exactly the right way to look at it -- these aren't just records, they're artifacts of a musical revolution that started in a small studio in Jamaica and changed global music. And 30 tunes on the wantlist is a great start. Just pace yourself -- set a monthly budget for vinyl so you don't go overboard, and prioritize the true grails first.

Also, if you're ever in a record store and they have a box of unlabeled or cheap Caribbean 45s, dig through it. That's where the surprises hide. Some of the best finds come from dusty bins that nobody's looked through in years. Happy hunting!
the dusty bin dig!! that's the dream scenario. ok you've got me HYPED I'm going to Kops Records this weekend. thank you for all this!! the collection starts NOW!!
Enjoy the hunt! Kops is a good starting spot -- tell them what you're looking for and they might have stuff in the back that's not on the floor yet. Good luck building the collection!
